faq.title: Questions?
faq.subtitle: Find answers to common questions about app compatibility and Windows on ARM.

items.question: How do I know if an app is compatible with Windows on ARM?
items.answer: Use the search bar on our homepage or browse the Apps section to check compatibility. Each app is tagged with its compatibility status — 'Yes' means it works, 'No' means it doesn't, and 'Unknown' means it hasn't been tested yet.

items.question: What is Prism and how does x86 emulation work?
items.answer: Prism is Microsoft's built-in translation technology that allows x86 and x64 applications to run on ARM processors. It translates instructions in real-time, enabling most existing Windows apps to work without modification.

items.question: What's the difference between 'Native' and 'Emulated'?
items.answer: Native apps are built specifically for ARM processors and run at full performance. Emulated apps run through Prism translation, which may have a small performance overhead but ensures compatibility with the vast majority of existing Windows software.

items.question: How do I report an app compatibility issue?
items.answer: You can report compatibility issues through our GitHub repository. Click 'Report an Issue' in the footer, and follow the template to provide details about the app and the issue you encountered.

items.question: Which Microsoft apps are fully native on ARM?
items.answer: Most Microsoft 365 apps including Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Outlook, Teams, Edge, Visual Studio Code, and many more are fully native on ARM. Check the Microsoft Apps section on our homepage for the complete list.

items.question: Can I run x64 applications on Windows on ARM?
items.answer: Yes! Windows on ARM supports x64 application emulation through Prism. Most x64 apps will run seamlessly, though native ARM apps will provide the best performance and battery life.
